response:
TCP and UDP are transport-layer protocols used to send data over IP networks. The main difference is that **TCP prioritizes reliability**, while **UDP prioritizes speed and simplicity**.

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Full name | Transmission Control Protocol | User Datagram Protocol |
| Connection type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Reliable: guarantees delivery, retransmits lost packets | Unreliable: no guarantee of delivery |
| Ordering | Guarantees data arrives in order | Packets may arrive out of order |
| Error checking | Yes, with recovery | Yes, but no recovery |
| Speed | Generally slower due to overhead | Generally faster with lower overhead |
| Overhead | Higher | Lower |
| Flow control | Yes | No |
| Congestion control | Yes | No built-in congestion control |
| Data format | Stream-based | Message/datagram-based |
| Handshake | Requires setup, e.g. TCP three-way handshake | No handshake |
| Broadcast/multicast | Not typically used | Supports broadcast and multicast |
| Common uses | Web browsing, email, file transfers, SSH | Video streaming, online gaming, VoIP, DNS |

### TCP example
TCP is used when correctness matters more than speed. For example, when downloading a file, every byte must arrive correctly and in order.

### UDP example
UDP is used when speed matters more than perfect reliability. For example, in a live video call, it is often better to drop a lost packet than to wait for retransmission and cause lag.

**In short:**  
- Use **TCP** when you need reliability and ordered delivery.  
- Use **UDP** when you need low latency and can tolerate some packet loss.
